summary:
The capture of Rome on 20 September 1870 and its subsequent designation as the capital of the Kingdom of Italy completed the territorial [[event:2533|Italian Unification]] process that had been underway for over two decades. Rome, the Eternal City and the seat of the papacy, had been the last major territory outside the Italian state — held by Pope Pius IX under French military protection since 1849.

The opportunity came with France's catastrophic defeat in the [[event:2535|Franco-Prussian War]]. When France recalled the garrison protecting Rome to defend French territory in August 1870, the Italian government hesitated but ultimately decided to act. Negotiations with the Pope failed; Pius IX refused to accept any diminution of his temporal authority. On 20 September 1870, Italian troops breached the Aurelian Walls at Porta Pia and entered Rome with minimal resistance after a token defense. The Italian Army suffered about 50 casualties; the papal defenders about 20.

A plebiscite confirmed Rome's annexation to Italy by an overwhelming majority. Rome became the capital in 1871. Pius IX, refusing to recognize the Italian state's legitimacy, declared himself 'Prisoner of the Vatican' and forbade Catholics to participate in Italian political life — a prohibition the papacy maintained for decades. This 'Roman Question' (the unresolved status of the papacy within the Italian state) was finally settled only by the Lateran Treaties of 1929 between Mussolini's government and the Holy See, which created the Vatican City as an independent state.

The capture of Rome was the culmination of Italian national unification but also the beginning of the difficult relationship between the Catholic Church and the Italian state that would mark Italian political life throughout the liberal period.
